data.title: Gold Bounces Despite The Oil Rally, A First Since The U.S.-Iran War
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The progressive explosion in crude prices, driven by failed hopes of a resolution to the Middle East conflict, has sent global assets struggling.

Investors and traders have been wondering how gold, as a safe haven and a traditional hedge against inflation, has been struggling so much throughout the entire month with bombs thrown around the Gulf.

Metals were actually large victims of their own prior success – After consistently rallying since October 2024, the asset class burnt its own wings.

Heavily chased commodities can see brutal swings when demand dampens: as money flows rushed into crude and the fundamental backdrop turned more hawkish, investors turned away from the shiny metals.

With most asset managers already long the asset class, as expressed in the February BofA Survey, no one was there to withhold the pressure.

But after a 25% correction in gold, we could see the bullion actually dominate its peers in the coming days – So why now?

A few technical patterns are providing some demand for it, like the 200-day MA acting as support (see below). Still, more importantly, gold is the only asset rallying while stocks, bonds, and other metals like silver and platinum are tumbling.

It has been a rough stretch for metals, but gold could rise from the ashes, particularly if investors start to price in further escalation (as seen with oil rallying back to $98 today).

After large corrections, it wouldn't be surprising to see the ultimate safe haven gain back some demand with the global economy projected to struggle.

Let's attack a high-level intraday analysis of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) as it attempts to break its curse amid the ongoing war.

Multi-timeframe analysis for Gold, starting from the Weekly to intraday

Gold Weekly timeframe

It would definitely not be crazy to assume that the correction for gold has come a long way, particularly as the selling erased most of the extension the metal did in 2026.

Weekly RSI is now back to neutral, allowing future movement to flow with less resistance.

Tumbling close to 25% to $4,100 lows, the Bullion rejected its support in an attempt to regain its major momentum pivot – Also forming a dragonfly doji (more bullish than bearish, but also a sign of hesitancy).

  • Breaking above $4,500 and closing there on the daily could easily relaunch the commodity towards $5,000 – with the current state of markets, that would mostly happen on a slow grind higher.
  • But with dojis, one also has to consider the downside – A daily close below the 50-week MA of $3,965 opens the door for a larger correction to $3,500 (less probable for now).

Gold Daily Chart

Looking closer, gold did find significant support at its 200-day MA, and after its previous day's failed dip below the key $4,400 level, bulls are now taking the advantage, particularly after a daily bullish RSI divergence.

Momentum still seems hesitant, and it will stay the same as long as prices remain within the $4,300 to $4,500 range – Any daily close above $4,550 (December 2025 record) would launch the commodity higher.

After the large correction, however, it would be difficult to see it suddenly explode (as said before, a progressive grind higher would make more technical sense).

Levels of interest for gold trading:

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,550 December 2025 record (intraday resistance)
  • February Wick Pivot $4,675 - $4,725 (bullish above)
  • $4,850 to $4,900 Key Resistance
  • $5,100 Pivotal Resistance
  • $5,400 mini-resistance

Support Levels:

  • Pivotal Support $4,355 – $4,400
  • Main Channel Lows Support & 200-Day MA $4,100
  • Next Support $3,880 to $4,000
  • $3,200 to $3,500 Major Support

Gold 2H Chart

Gold is getting back to a more bullish momentum from seller exhaustion – currently contained by its 2H 50-period MA on the intraday, bulls will want to push above in order to materialize a more significant rebound.

Keep a close eye on the triangle formation for upward and downward breakout boundaries – when markets reopen next week, traders can expect significant movement; hence, watch out for your size and orders!

Original Post

data.title: Circle: Buying The Clarity Act Confusion
data.description:
I have covered Circle Internet Group (CRCL) twice here on Seeking Alpha. Both times I leaned toward a cautious Hold. I initiated coverage just after the IPO at ~$83, where there was an immediate ~$75 premium to tangible book value following the IPO, which made a Buy difficult to justify at the time. I revisited CRCL in November last year around $100, following the post-IPO surge to ~$300 and the eventual retrace below ~$100, caused by the combination of decelerating USDC (USDC-USD) circulation, rising OpEx guidance, and the way the market viewed a business that still generated over 90% of its top line from T-bill interest income with the interest rates falling and speculations of further rate cuts. Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) decline in early February from its all-time high added sentiment pressure surrounding crypto assets and crypto-linked stocks.

CRCL has since recovered and staged a move towards $150 last month after Q4 earnings beat and positive guidance for FY26. That recovery had already begun to fade, with the stock drifting back to ~$126, before CRCL lost an additional 20% on Tuesday. The latest drop was tied to the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act’s stablecoin yield restriction in the latest draft, which has brought CRCL back to the same price range as my follow-up last November.

At this juncture, and for the first time, I am upgrading CRCL to a Buy despite this week's market reaction to the legislative draft, with the caveat that a leg lower from the current price is still probable before the upward thesis plays out.

What the Market Misread, and Why CRCL Deserves an Upgrade

Tuesday’s Clarity Act selloff seems to have been an emotional, overblown reaction by the market, in my view. The Clarity Act's draft language states that it would bar rewards on passive stablecoin balances and prohibit structures "economically equivalent to interest." The market's immediate interpretation was that Circle's revenue model had been legislated away.

The distinction I think matters is that Circle earns reserve income on the pool of T-bills and cash equivalents backing USDC in circulation. Circle does not pay yield directly to USDC holders or run a platform that does. USDC is, and has always been, a non-yield-bearing stablecoin in that sense. The Clarity Act restricts platforms from offering yield to holders, but it does not restrict stablecoin issuers from earning income on the reserve pool.

But there is an indirect threat to stablecoins in general, which could stifle Circle’s stablecoin growth and adoption and move crypto users towards activity-based yield-generating assets as passive yield is being obviously stifled by the Act. If competing yield-bearing instruments become more attractive compared to non-yielding stablecoins like USDC, stablecoin adoption could slow, making circulation of a stablecoin like USDC see contraction, and the reserve base that generates that income would shrink with it.

Beyond that indirect headwind to stablecoin adoption and supply growth, I see Circle quietly positioning itself on the right side of legislation and curtailing the risks to its stablecoin presence in the U.S. Circle filed its OCC application in June last year. In December, the OCC granted Circle conditional approval to establish First National Digital Currency Bank, N.A. (which made Circle one of five crypto-native firms to receive such approval), the first multi-firm conditional approval of its kind in the OCC's history. Though the approval is conditional, not final. Per the OCC's own letter, final authorization to commence business will not be granted until all pre-opening requirements are met, including capital, governance, and risk-management standards. Circle is currently in that pre-opening phase.

Once it is fully approved, First National Digital Currency Bank would operate as a federally regulated trust bank under OCC oversight and would manage the USDC Reserve directly on behalf of Circle's U.S. issuer. That in itself would eliminate third-party custodian costs and bring reserve management in-house and will likely have a direct impact on OpEx margins. Remember how higher OpEx guidance was one of the main reasons CRCL dropped in November; so operationally, this in itself is a big deal. On ~$75 billion in reserves, even a modest reduction in custody costs flows directly to the bottom line.

And there is more to why the banking precedent matters directly for Circle's path through the Clarity Act. The Federal Reserve pays interest on reserve balances held by depository institutions; this is a practice Congress authorized through the Financial Services Regulatory Relief Act of 2006, which came into effect in October 2008. The Fed's own policy page states it plainly that banks and other depository institutions hold deposits with the Federal Reserve known as reserve balances, and the Fed pays interest on those deposits, with the rate set by the Federal Reserve Board to implement monetary policy decisions.

That economic structure is the same as Circle's, which follows the pattern of holding reserve assets against liabilities and earning the interest. The difference today is just legal classification. Circle is a stablecoin issuer, not a depository institution. The OCC charter is the mechanism that I believe would close that. If Circle had not taken the steps for such regulatory reclassification by now, I'd have said the market reaction to the Clarity Act’s draft earlier this week was fair and in place.

A federally chartered trust bank earning interest on reserves it manages directly won’t have any ambiguous representations of its operations, because it is doing precisely what Congress authorized in 2008. The Clarity Act's yield restriction language was written to govern stablecoin distributors, not federally regulated reserve managers. Circle's OCC approval, once finalized, is the most credible way to make the "economically equivalent to interest" argument legally irrelevant to Circle's core business model. If we go by liquidity alone, I think Circle is on track to gain the OCC approval. The OCC typically requires de novo trust banks (newly chartered banks without a prior operating history as a national bank) to maintain between $6 million and $25 million in Tier 1 capital. Circle has a massive $75 billion+ AUM footprint, so I think the OCC likely pegged their specific requirement at the top end of $25 million. And with $1.53 billion in cash and cash equivalents as of Q4 FY25 end (separate from the over $75 billion USDC reserve), Circle is already exceeding the capital requirement by nearly 50x.

The OCC also has a 180-day operating liquidity requirement, which Circle also clearly beats with the current financial standing. The requirement mandates that certain specialized financial institutions, such as national crypto banks or fintech banks, hold eligible liquid assets sufficient to cover at least 180 days of the bank's expected average OpEx. Circle's projected 180-day OpEx is ~$285 million (based on FY26 OpEx guidance of $570 million to $585 million). Circle's current liquidity position covers this by over 4x. And to top that, Circle’s leverage ratio has been pristine, which is also an essential green light for the OCC approval.

Beyond the zeal to be “regulatory correct,” other growth levers worth tracking for Circle include the European market penetration of EURC. The Clarity Act is U.S.-based legislation. MiCA, on the other hand, is already enforced across the EU. That enforcement was the cause of Tether's euro-backed stablecoin being withdrawn, creating what I’d call a "compliance vacuum" left by non-MiCA-compliant stablecoin issuers, seeing Circle’s EURC grow by 2,700% between July 2024 (when MiCA was just implemented for Euro-backed stablecoins) and June 2025. Circle's EURC now holds the default position as the regulated euro-backed stablecoin for on-chain finance in Europe, with EURC now commanding over 50% of Europe's stablecoin market share. All of these tailwinds are not directly affected by the Clarity Act, being another reason why I feel the market overreacted.

Then the Arc mainnet, which is Circle’s purpose-built blockchain for enterprise financial activity with USDC as the gas token currently in testnet with several institutional heavyweights like Apollo (APO), BNY Mellon (BK), and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) among the testnet participants. Mainnet launch is slated for later this year, according to Circle’s internal roadmap. When the mainnet launches, fee-based revenue from payment and capital markets transactions on Arc would become Circle's best response yet to the interest rate sensitivity concern I raised in both of my previous articles on Circle.

Similarly, Circle’s USYC (a tokenized Treasury fund) is Circle's "yield-bearing" answer when the Clarity Act is finally enacted. Since the draft legislation targets passive rewards on stablecoins, I believe capital will pivot to assets like USYC to capture Treasury yields in a compliant, tokenized wrapper. Here, it is a zero-sum game in Circle’s favor. If the yield restriction holds and stablecoin circulation drops, USYC could effectively recapture that liquidity flight. As of early this month, USYC crossed $2.2 billion in assets under management (now at $2.6 billion) and recently overtook BlackRock’s BUIDL to become the largest tokenized Treasury product in the world.

How I Value Circle Today

With all the tailwinds I have mentioned so far, from EURC dominance in Europe, USYC dominance among tokenized treasuries, the drive to be regulatory compliant at the federal level, and Circle's efficient operations (having reported over 50% EBITDA margins in Q4 FY25), I am re-rating Circle as a high-growth fintech infrastructure with an unassailable moat in digital dollars.

On an EV/NTM (next twelve months) Sales basis, USDC has averaged $75.2 billion in circulation from the start of the year to date, up 6% since Q4 last year (supply hit 78.7 billion USDC valued at $78.9 billion last week). Applying a 3.5% reserve return rate (accounting for further rate cuts likelihood) to an $80 billion average circulation assumption that only grows by 1.6% over the next fiscal year would yield $2.8 billion in gross NTM revenue ($80 billion average circulation × 0.035 yield = $2.8 billion revenue). Circle itself has given a 40% medium-term CAGR for USDC circulation in FY26; thus, my move to $80 billion is actually super conservative.

The main attraction for Circle is the efficient operations demonstrated with Q4’s 50% EBITDA margin. As the OCC charter finalizes and brings custody in-house, I expect these margins to remain top-quartile even if interest rates go lower.

I’ll be a bit aggressive with Circle here and give it a fintech infrastructure multiple of 12x, which would imply a market cap of ~$33.6 billion ($2.8 billion NTM Revenue x 12x EV/Sales). With shares outstanding currently ~242 million, this brings my per-share value to ~$139 ($33.6 billion Market Cap / 242 million Shares ≈ $139). Payment infrastructure peers are currently trading around 6x to 7x, but I believe Circle’s premium is justified by that 50% EBITDA profile and the regulatory moat it has built. My ~$139 target price would be a ~40% upside from the current price of ~$100.

Risks

With all the tailwinds mentioned throughout this piece, we can't ignore the fact that Circle still derives a majority of its revenue from interest income on T-bills. While I've also mentioned EURC and Arc as growth levers that could potentially keep the top line from diminishing if interest rates fall lower, the market perception and reaction to rate changes as it applies to CRCL is beyond Circle’s and any analyst's control. If the market continues to tie Circle’s valuation to rates, it means rate cuts could see CRCL de-rate.

In addition to that, some of the catalysts I've mentioned are speculative. The adoption of the Arc blockchain when it launches isn't guaranteed. The OCC charter outcome is also at the discretion and timeline of regulators and isn't a guaranteed outcome.

Takeaway

Circle is not a yield distributor, and the business model has not just been outlawed by the Clarity Act. Beyond the Clarity Act, Circle boasts ample tailwinds elsewhere, with EURC dominating in Europe, the Arc blockchain being stress-tested by big institutional players, and potential for final authorization for depository operations from the OCC charter.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Argan's Exuberant Momentum Meets Excellent Monetization - Maintain Hold
data.description:
I previously covered Argan, Inc. (AGX) in January 2026, discussing why I had reiterated my Hold rating then, thanks to the mixed return prospects arising from the baked-in growth premium in its stock prices/valuations.

This was worsened by the potential volatility from the construction company's lumpy revenue recognition and the stock's elevated short interest ratio of 4.71% then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold rating for the AGX stock here, given its mixed risk/reward prospects at current inflated levels, worsened by the overly done upward momentum already triggering the increasingly expensive valuations compared to its historical averages/peers.

This is despite their beneficiary status during the multi-year AI and electrification capex trends, as observed in the expanding multi-year backlog, the richer margins, and the healthier balance sheet.

AGX Proves Their AI/Electrification Boom Beneficiary Status

Since my last Hold rating, AGX has remained volatile indeed, as observed in the stock charting a new high around the $492s in March 2026 before losing much of those gains to retest the $410s pre-FQ4'26 earnings call.

Well, this is before the market cheered their outsized performance metrics in the FQ4'26 earnings call on March 26, 2026, with the stock experiencing an excellent recovery by +6.3% to $437.48 post-market.

For reference, AGX's beneficiary status during the multi-year AI and electrification capex trends has been exemplified through the robust FQ4'26 earnings call, with:

  • revenues of $262.05M (+12.7% YoY),
  • gross profit margins of 25% (+4.5 points YoY/+7 from FY2019 levels of 17%),
  • adj EBITDA margins of 21.4% (+4.5 points YoY/+10.6 from FY2019 levels of 10.8%), and
  • adj EPS of $3.47 (+56.3% YoY),

Here is where their construction expertise in the power sector has worked out handsomely for them, particularly in the natural gas-fired power plants and renewable energy facilities, attributed to the accelerated power grid build-out in the US, from $208B in 2025 to the projected sum of $1.1T by 2030, expanding at a CAGR of +39.5%.

Combined with the US power system limits during the multi-year data center boom, with S&P Global already expecting the US data center power consumption to grow from 57.68 GW in 2025 to 103.53 GW in 2030, I am of the opinion that AGX is likely to benefit from the elevated utility upgrading/construction activities moving forward.

This is especially since the construction company has already reported an outsized new contract value of $2.5B in FY2026, with it directly contributing to the growing multi-year backlog of $2.92B (+114.7% YoY).

Most importantly, AGX's intensified focus on their electrification opportunities has been highly strategic, given the Power segment's notably richer gross profit margins of 22.3% in FY2026 (+5.7 points YoY) compared to the Industrial segment at 12.1% (-1.2 points YoY) and the Teledata segment at 19.5% (-4.3 points YoY).

These numbers lend credibility to the company's elevated exposure to the utility sector, as it comprises 93% of its overall backlog in FY2026 (in line YoY) and 78% of its FQ4'26 revenues (-7 points YoY).

Given that part of its backlog stretches through 2029, it is unsurprising that the market has rewarded the AGX stock post FQ4'26 earnings call, with the richer profit margins underscoring their improving pricing power and operating leverage at a time of capex boom.

If anything, the management has hinted at further contract wins over the next 12 to 20 months, attributed to 4 of its ongoing projects expected to reach completion in 2026 and their expanded execution capacity of up to 12 simultaneous jobs.

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain excellent despite AGX not offering any forward guidance, with the company expected to report robust top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +17.6%/+6.8% through FY2029.

These numbers build upon the historical growth profile at a 10Y CAGR of +8.6%/+4.8%, despite the moderation from the 5Y CAGR of +19.2%/+45.1%, respectively.

As a result of AGX's increasingly profitable growth prospects aided by the excellent top/bottom-line visibility from the expanding backlog over the next three years or so, I can understand why the market has gotten extremely exuberant, as observed in the rallying stock prices post-market.

AGX Remains A Hold Here

For now, AGX's recovery post-earnings call further proves the bullish uptrend support line established since early 2024, with the stock likely to remain well supported along the 50-day moving averages moving forward.

The exuberance embedded in its stock prices is also why AGX continues to boast notably premium FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 36.30x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 52.20x.

This is compared to the 1Y mean of 21.19x/36.50x, the 5Y mean of 9.60x/22.31x, and the sector median of 11.86x/19.21x, respectively, with it apparent that the market is rather euphoric about their multi-year, profitable growth prospects.

When comparing AGX's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 36.30x (5Y mean of 22.31x) and the FY2026 adj. EBITDA margins of 17.2% (+6.4 points from FY2019 levels of 10.8%) to its construction peers with data-center exposure, including:

  • Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL) at 21.95x (10.83x)/FY2025 margins of 20.2% (+14.6 points from FY2019 levels of 5.6%),
  • Dycom Industries, Inc. (DY) at 13.16x (10.80x)/FY2026 margins of 13.3% (+2.8 points from FY2019 levels of 10.5%),
  • Ameresco, Inc. (AMRC) at 14.08x (19.88x)/FY2025 margins of 12.2% (+1.7 points from FY2019 levels of 10.5%), and
  • Primoris Services Corporation (PRIM) at 14.97x (9.33x)/FY2025 margins of 6.9% (-0.3 points from FY2019 levels of 7.2%), respectively,

... I am of the opinion that AGX's premium valuations have been overdone in comparison to its peers' adj. EBITDA margin and adj. EBITDA margin expansion from the FY2019 levels.

On the one hand, part of the premium may be attributed to AGX's ability to deliver these impressive numbers with an increasingly richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $894.98M in FQ4'26 (+70.4% YoY) and the net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of -5.49x in FY2026 (given the net cash position) compared to STRL at -0.19x, DY at 2.85x, AMRC at 7.63x, and PRIM at -0.12x.

On the other hand, it goes without saying that the overly done rally has already triggered:

  • the widening gap to their tangible book value per share at $31.02 (+31.2% YoY),
  • the moderating forward dividend yields of 0.46% (against the 5Y mean of 3.17% and the sector median of 1.48%), and
  • the minimal margin of safety to my long-term price target of $264.80 (based on the 5Y P/E mean of 22.31x (near to the sector median of 19.21x) and the consensus FY2029 adj EPS estimates of $11.87),

... with it presenting AGX's mixed risk/reward prospects at current inflated levels, worsened by the unlikely to be sustainable upward momentum given the increasingly expensive valuations compared to its historical averages/peers, resulting in my reiterated Hold rating here.

Do not chase this overpriced stock.

data.title: Nvidia's Recent Correction Is An Opportunity, Not A Warning
data.description:
It's an interesting time for investors in Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), the world's largest company and leading provider of chips and software solutions serving the massive AI frenzy we've seen in recent years.

On one hand, investors appear concerned about the potential for an AI capex cliff, as cloud providers who lack significant AI-attributable revenues may decide to slow their spending on new AI chipsets.

On the other hand, the emergence of always-on AI agents like OpenClaw highlights the potential for long-term durable inference demand across domains. As compute capacity begins to translate directly to revenue, agentic workflows could drive immediate, significant ROI for cloud providers, which in turn could boost sales for NVDA's new Rubin hardware.

Since my last article on Nvidia, shares have entered correction territory, down roughly 17% from highs - even following a relatively bullish GTC conference:

In this article, I wanted to take a deeper look at the price action and understand whether this recent dip represents an opportunity for new capital to get involved in the story or a warning to investors that the AI cycle may be coming to a close.

Today, I'll touch on recent updates with the stock, highlight the valuation, and make the case that shares of NVDA should be scooped up at this price, not discarded.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

Financials

At a high level, in case you're unfamiliar with Nvidia (which seems impossible at this point), the company is essentially a semiconductor company that sells GPUs and other chipsets to cloud infrastructure companies, other businesses, countries, and individuals around the world. Nvidia's hardware has consistently led the pack when it comes to performance and total cost of operations, making it the preferred choice for those developing models and building out next-generation data centers. At the core of the story is also Nvidia's CUDA programming framework, which is the oldest and most comprehensive library of GPU-optimized AI software in existence.

Together, the hardware and software products form a virtuous cycle. As companies buy and deploy Nvidia products in their data centers (and elsewhere), they tend to use CUDA software to get the most out of the hardware. As companies expand and replace old chipsets, replacing the software stack creates high switching costs and an economic moat for Nvidia, which should hold up even in the event that competitors like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) catch up in terms of hardware performance.

Right now, the Nvidia earnings situation is at a crossroads. For years, companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META) have purchased hundreds of billions of dollars of GPUs from Nvidia. As investments have deepened but AI-attributable revenue has trailed, many have argued that we will eventually reach hyperscaler "sufficiency"—where new investments in hardware become incremental, not ongoing. When this happens, Nvidia's growing revenues may peak and enter a downcycle.

It's a valid case that deserves consideration, and in the event that new agentic frameworks hadn't come out in recent months, I may feel obliged to agree. At the end of the day, capex only makes sense when it truly generates long-term ROI. At the scale and size of the current spend, in theory, we should expect to see cloud revenues growing at a faster rate than they are.

However, more importantly, I believe that we are on the verge of a new wave of the AI transformation, as AI agents begin to disseminate across society.

Programs like OpenClaw for personal computing, Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude Code for software development, and Salesforce's (CRM) Agentforce for sales organizations - just to name a few examples - stand to massively boost demand for always-on AI inference. In the short term, I expect that this will be a relative blip versus current AI inference workloads, which primarily center around chatbots like OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT and generative AI programs like NanoBanana that create images and videos.

In the medium to longer term, though, I expect that agentic workflows will make up almost all inference demand, as individuals and businesses set up agents to help make them more efficient and free up their time.

Equipped with a phone number, an email, and a credit card, there's a surprising number of things that agents can do - and we're only in the first inning of this evolution. Long-term, as human workflows become upended by agents that operate at a fraction of the cost, I expect to see a portion of overall corporate labor budgets diverted towards AI.

This has significant revenue implications for cloud companies. Here's what Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia, said on the recent earnings call:

We have now seen the inflection of agentic AI and the usefulness of agents across the world and enterprises everywhere. You're seeing incredible compute demand because of it. In this new world of AI, compute is revenues. Without compute, there's no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there's no way to grow revenues. So in this new world of AI, compute equals revenues.

On the bottom line, this has significant implications for Nvidia, who may be able to keep growing revenues as inference workloads expand - even as chips become more and more efficient at servicing this demand.

Right now, analysts are currently projecting significant revenue growth for Nvidia over the next few years, backed by the CEO's claim that the company sees $1 trillion in Blackwell/Rubin demand through 2027:

Now, I don't know if you guys feel the same way, but $500 billion is an enormous amount of revenue. Well, I'm here to tell you that right now where I stand - a few short months after GTC DC, one year after last GTC - right here where I stand, I see through 2027, at least $1 trillion.

All told, agents are a significant development within the broader AI story and one that should benefit Nvidia directly.

Valuation

At the same time, there is a ceiling for total spend here in the medium term, and over the long term we'll likely see a down year or two as AI changes are digested amidst broader society, we have some societal pushback against agents, and improvements in inference speed reduce the need for structural buildout.

As a result, I'm projecting a lower-than-average steady-state multiple for the stock in the coming years of 24x earnings, as opposed to trying to predict when exactly the story may hit a snag. This is lower than Nvidia's long-term earnings multiple, but with the company seeing revenue acceleration slow, I'm trying to model what a potential "exit" multiple may be for investors in the future:

If you apply this "steady state" valuation framework to the estimates I referenced earlier, we're looking at total returns of roughly 15% to 16% through the end of the decade, assuming some buybacks:

Given that I view this multiple as relatively conservative, and Nvidia still pencils out towards delivering mid-teens total returns, it's enough for a Buy rating for me:

However, for those who want to reduce risk while potentially increasing their return profile, I believe that selling put options is an excellent way to play the stock.

By selling July 17th put options with a $160 strike and a credit of $10.48 per share, investors can generate 7% cash-on-cash returns over the next 111 days, annualizing to a 23% yield:

If assigned, investors stand to get into the Nvidia story at only 19.3x forward earnings, which I view as particularly attractive. If we assume that the above estimates hold, this also implies significantly improved annualized returns over the next few years versus a vanilla Buy case.

As such, I believe selling put options in Nvidia is probably the most optimized way to deploy capital from a risk-reward perspective.

Overall, I am bullish on NVDA.

Risks

Of course, there are some risks worth pointing out here for investors.

First, as I talked about in detail, the AI capex cliff is a real consideration. Even if long-term demand for Nvidia chips stays intact and even grows, we could see a cyclical downturn in growth purely due to spending timing decisions made at Nvidia's key customers' purchasing departments. If Nvidia does not continue its history of beating analyst estimates, the stock may be in for a negative surprise.

Longer-term, Nvidia faces significant competition from firms like AMD (AMD), Intel (INTC), Broadcom (AVGO), and others, who produce everything from GPUs to networking chips and hardware. As these products improve, Nvidia's lead may lessen, leading to reduced growth and margins.

Finally, executing a sales ramp for Vera Rubin represents a significant execution challenge, especially given the size and monetary weight of the deliverables. Nvidia will need to ensure it keeps everything tight from an execution perspective in the next few years, or it could impact the financials and investor returns.

Summary

Overall, though, I view the balance of risks and rewards as heavily favored towards buyers of NVDA stock. While it's worth considering when - over the long run - the investment cycle may peak, the emergence of agentic workloads and the current price make Nvidia stock an attractive option for now.

Hence, my buy rating.

Cheers~

data.title: BlackRock Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio management

Reid Menge, Sally Du, CFA

Top 10 holdings (%)

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) 13.97
Microsoft (MSFT) 8.80
Amazon.com (AMZN) 8.05
Apple (AAPL) 5.88
Meta Platforms Inc (META) 5.06
Alphabet (GOOGL) 4.95
Broadcom Inc (AVGO) 4.89
Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) 4.57
Tesla Inc (TSLA) 3.25
Oracle Corporation (ORCL) 3.05

Investment approach

Invests in a portfolio of equity securities that fund management believes have shown above-average growth rates in earnings over the long term.

Commentary as of 12/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -1.02% (Institutional shares)(MAFGX) and -1.08% (Investor A shares, without sales charge)(MDFGX) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were investment decisions in the health care, financials, and information technology (IT) sectors. The largest detractors from relative returns were investment decisions in the communication services, industrials, and real estate sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the IT and industrials sectors, and reduced its exposures to the financials and consumer discretionary sectors.

Contributors

Stock selection in the health care sector, namely in the pharmaceuticals industry, was the largest contributor to relative performance. In the financials sector, stock selection in the capital markets industry proved beneficial. Selection decisions in the software industry, within the IT sector, also boosted relative results.

Detractors

The largest detractor was security selection in the communication services sector, notably in the interactive media & services industry. In the industrials sector, selection decisions in the aerospace & defense industry hindered performance. Another meaningful detractor included stock selection in the real estate sector, namely in the real estate management & development industry.

Further insight

U.S. equities enter 2026 with strong momentum after another standout year. Despite questions about enthusiasm driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and global performance dispersion, the macroeconomic backdrop remains supportive, with Federal Reserve interest rate cuts, resilient consumers, upgraded growth forecasts, and policy tailwinds boosting competitiveness. Earnings revisions favor U.S. companies, and leadership is broadening beyond the so-called mega-caps stocks, creating opportunities for active investors. Structural themes – AI, health care innovation, defense technology, “Gen Z” (typically people born in the United States between the late 1990s and early 2010s) consumer trends, and digitization – continue to shape long-term growth. With mega-cap dominance less certain, we emphasize an earnings-first approach focused on differentiated multiyear earnings-per-share trajectories, pricing power, and durable secular catalysts to drive returns.
