data.title: Realty Income: No Real Reasons To Panic Now (Upgrade)
data.description:
Realty Income: Another Timely Opportunity

Real estate investors are in for another reckoning. In just over three weeks, most of the gains for the year have been lost, with the real estate sector now trading flat year-to-date, and REIT investors have also suffered the brunt of the risk-off sentiments worsening, as investors react to the uncertainties linked to the current Iranian War.

For Realty Income Corporation (O) investors, I explained in my last update that I no longer saw O stock as attractive enough to maintain a bullish view, and that downgrade turned out to be timely. Even though management provided what I thought was relatively solid guidance at the recent Q4 earnings, the same-store NOI growth of just 1.1% also showcased just how challenging it is, as the net lease REIT is arguably suffering from the law of large numbers. The acquisition blueprint of the company needs a highly constructive climate to drive AFFO growth further over time but has been hampered by the challenges in the public equities market in the past few years.

Nevertheless, I thought the guidance presented in Q4 for $4.40 in AFFO at the midpoint still seems like a pretty okay one. The important point for me is that it still represents an inflection from FY2025, suggesting that management is confident of a more conducive environment for acquisitive growth this year.

Realty Income: Interest Rates Headwinds Returning

However, the recent tumble would have caused mixed feelings among investors, as now they start to ask questions about whether the current environment can be characterized as reasonable, especially since economists have stopped asking how many interest rate cuts we are going to have this year, as the ongoing war has completely flipped the narrative. In contrast, we are now asking how many interest rate hikes we could possibly end up with this year and over the next 12 months.

This obviously has potentially significant implications, because if we were to fall into a stagflationary environment, it would possibly be one of the worst economic dynamics that Realty Income could possibly ask for, since the 10Y yield (US10Y) is considered a proxy for income investors to juxtapose as spreads widen.

However, while the market will reasonably need to adjust the valuation multiples of O stock lower, taking into account these changes in interest rate expectations, it may not necessarily impact Realty Income’s business model as long as the war impact isn’t expected to persist.

Realty Income Has Strengthened Its Funding Sources

In fact, as highlighted by management at a recent March conference, one of the key chokepoints that Realty Income assessed was their so-called “one source of equity capital, which was the public markets.” However, Realty Income has since mitigated and diversified the financing sources, augmented by the recent partnerships with GIC and Blackstone, providing the company with a sustainable source of funding from highly regarded real estate institutional investors with extremely deep pockets and long-term investing horizons.

This affords the confidence for O to proceed with a more ambitious acquisition program, especially since the company noted that while it assessed >$120 billion of potential opportunities in its pipeline in 2025, they only closed $6 billion worth. Hence, I think Realty Income sees a tremendous opportunity to tap this source of funding to not only expand its investment horizon but also provide it the financial capabilities to close the right deals as they come. The company is keen to avoid unpalatable scenarios in missing out on solid opportunities again if the public equity markets were not conducive, hampering their equity funding raising, even if they could spot solid investment opportunities in their core categories.

And I think this is even more important as Realty Income looks to Europe for growth, given the current fragmentation in the market that should benefit big players like O, with their financing capacity and rock-solid credit rating.

After all, the company focuses on more defensive setups, widely considered as non-discretionary. The rental base is accrued mainly from these “nondiscretionary service-oriented or low price point businesses that have historically performed well across economic cycles.” Which is why I think Realty Income is confident in its ability to invest through the market cycles while also delivering an ever-growing dividend payout. However, that doesn't mean that they will not be affected by market cycles and downcast investing sentiments.

So, while I don’t think that O will be immune from a further selloff, I'm willing to bet that the market will accord more confidence to its guidance, helping the REIT to achieve what it believes to be transacting “$10 billion to $12 billion a year on a consistent basis” moving forward. I think those asset manager deals should mark a start, paving the way for potentially bigger partnerships to be inked either with GIC or Blackstone or other asset managers. It’s interesting to note that Realty Income did expand on the Blackstone deal, as management underscored the success of the “first deal” on Bellagio, which also helped them to nail the “second deal, which was the CityCenter (deal).”

I think Realty Income’s ability to raise >$1.5 billion through third-party equity programs corroborates the confidence placed in the team while also augmenting the prospects into newer growth vectors, working with these mega asset managers. And with Europe’s exposure nearing almost 20% of its annualized base rent, I believe it portends well for Realty Income in the next couple of years to meet or surpass the analyst estimates more confidently, with 2025 likely the low point in its AFFO per share growth momentum.

Buy Or Sell O Stock?

Nevertheless, while the recent selloff may seem daunting, we should not misconstrue what appears to me as a rather quick digestion event, as the market quickly went into a risk-off posture.

I think what's crucial to note is that the market hasn't overturned the bullish narrative yet. Note that I observe that O stock is still in an uptrend configuration, and as long as that bullish directional bias holds, we should see income investors returning to support the recent sharp pullback. Moreover, O’s forward AFFO per share multiple has dropped back to 13.7x, with the forward yield now at about 5.5%. It represents a slight discount to the sector median (<6%), indicating that the market has somewhat de-risked the opportunity.

Of course, if the selling pressure continues to persist, then obviously we have to expect a possible test of the sub-$60 levels, but I will consider that an even more attractive opportunity for investors to return on board.

Therefore, I will say that the price action and valuations for Realty Income stock have aligned more closely with what I will consider typically as an attractive buying point.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Geopolitical Tension Pressures Stocks, While Earnings Still Offer Support
data.description:
By James Picerno

The stock market’s decline since the Iran war started isn’t surprising, but some market observers have been puzzled by the gradual, orderly slide in equities to date. Why hasn’t the conflict triggered a deeper correction? Robust earnings may be a factor.

The S&P 500’s current drawdown is -7.2%, which is still relatively modest by historical standards. No one knows if the decline will deepen, or when the war will end, but solid earnings data is a likely reason why the correction, so far, is still within a “normal” range.

Earnings are a key - if not the primary - driver of equity returns through time, which suggests that recent gains on this front, along with upbeat expectations for the near-term outlook, may have helped limit the downside for stocks.

The research firm FactSet estimates that the equity market’s year-over-year earnings growth rate through this year’s first quarter is 12.5%. “If 12.5% is the actual growth rate for the quarter, it will mark the sixth straight quarter of double‑digit (year over year) earnings growth reported by the index,” writes the senior analyst.

For recent historical perspective, consider the stock market’s performance (S&P 500 Index) in context with earnings. Unsurprisingly, equities have been rising in line with earnings growth. If the bullish forecasts for earnings are accurate, the growth trend may provide support for stocks and help minimize the war-related downside risk.

Market volatility in the short term can spike for a variety of reasons, and so, it’s never clear how much support earnings can provide over a few weeks or months. As the renowned value investor Ben Graham famously observed, “In the short run, the market is a voting machine, but in the long run it is a weighing machine.”

Wall Street remains generally upbeat on S&P earnings for 2026. The question is how much of a haircut could be brewing if the war runs longer than the optimistic views suggest?

Analysts at Barclays this week wrote: “We believe the US continues to offer stronger nominal growth than other major economies and a secular growth engine in technology that shows few signs of stopping,” the bank’s strategists advised. “We are incrementally bullish on US equities, though the road likely stays bumpy until we turn a corner.”

The war is still the elephant in the room in terms of risk factors. The sooner the conflict ends, the higher the confidence that the growth trend for earnings can continue with minimal damage.

Another key variable to watch is economic growth. The Atlanta Fed’s current nowcast for the upcoming first-quarter GDP report points to a moderate rebound in output following the sluggish rise in the fourth quarter.

If the war ends soon, and the economic headwinds from the conflict are relatively mild and short-lived, the odds will remain favorable that corporate earnings will continue to rise and provide a degree of ballast for the market in the months ahead.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Organogenesis Facing Hard Times As It Adjusts To CMS Rule Changes
data.description:
This is my eighth Organogenesis (ORGO) article, following most recently 12/2025's "Organogenesis: High-Tech Skin Substitute Company On Sale". I have been emboldened rather than discouraged by its ~50% drop since On Sale was published. The big question is whether it's a bargain or a falling knife.

After smashing Q4/2025 revenue expectations guidance coldcocked Organogenesis shares

Organogenesis reported a superlative Q4/2025 with revenues that handily surpassed analyst's expectations; the release reported:

  1. revenue of $225.1 million (+77.7% Y/Y), a beat of $52.42 million and
  2. Q4 GAAP EPS of $0.24, a beat of $0.03.

Its release reporting this great news then added a spoiler in terms of the very important outlook for the upcoming year 2026. In this regard the release turned downbeat when it guided expectations for 2026 as stated below:

Fiscal Year 2026 Outlook:

For the year ending December 31, 2026, the Company expects:

  • Total net revenue between $350.0 million and $420.0 million, representing a decline in the range of 25% to 38%, as compared to total net revenue of $564.2 million for the year ended December 31, 2025. (emphasis added)
    • The 2026 total net revenue guidance range assumes a significant year-over-year decline in revenue for the three months ending March 31, 2026, and strong quarter-over-quarter growth in the second, third and fourth quarters of fiscal year 2026.

A revenue drop of up to 38% is a huge deal, particularly in today's economic climate garbled by the war in the Middle East. Insofar as the release was issued nearly 2/3rds of the way through Q1/2026 on 2/26/2026, its Q1/2026 was mostly already in the bag at the time it published this outlook. In other words, its outlook for the quarter was likely based on more than dour speculation.

It's no easy task to wrap one's mind around this report's significance for Organogenesis' expected subsequent quarterly revenues. Its annual revenues for 2026 were estimated at $350-$420 million—representing expected midpoint revenues of $385 million. This decline was to consist of revenue decline for Q1/2026 followed by strong quarter-over-quarter growth in the ensuing quarters of fiscal year 2026.

Its 2025 quarterly revenues came in at:

  1. Q1/2025 — $86.7 million, with a ~16% increase to;
  2. Q2/2025 — $100.8 million, with ~50% increase to;
  3. Q3/2025 — $150.5 million, with ~50% increase to;
  4. Q4/2025 — $225.1 million an overall increase of ~160%.

Its outlook is for a rough continuation of its 2025 revenue progression, just starting from a far lower base.

During the Q4/2025 Call, CFO Francisco added significant gloss to its outlook for 2026. He reiterated expectations for 2026 revenue declines of 25% to 38%. He noted the revenue drop as primarily occurring in Q1/2026, with a year-over-year Q1 expected decline of 50%.

This would put Q1/2026 revenue at ~$43 million. Going forward into Q2/2026 and beyond, he noted:

We expect to drive strong sequential growth in the second quarter, resulting in first half revenue declines of approximately 30% to 35%. We expect to deliver strong sequential revenue growth in both the third and fourth quarter of 2026, which we expect will result in positive adjusted EBITDA, particularly in the fourth quarter, where we expect to drive high teens adjusted EBITDA margins.

CMS decisions are roiling Organogenesis's Q1/2026

As noted above, Organogenesis's magnificent string of rising revenues throughout 2025 is expected to screech to a halt when it reports Q1/2026 revenues. During its Q4/2025 earnings Call, CEO Gillheeney was careful to remain upbeat about the general direction of CMS decisions.

He noted that CMS had:

... cited the clinical differentiation of PMA products and supports higher payment for the category to encourage innovation in the space. Their comments indicate that PMA products were never part of the problem and understand the higher development and manufacturing costs require sufficient reimbursement, not only to sustain the market availability of Apligraf and other high-value PMA products, but also to introduce new PMA products in the future.

Unfortunately, this recognition appears unlikely to impact skin substitute product prices in 2026. Rather, it is indicative of intent to do so effectively in subsequent years based on anticipated market forces impacting these products. To be sure, Gillheeney seems optimistic that PMA products will receive favorable treatment.

Whether that in fact plays out on a time frame that boosts Organogenesis's revenues for upcoming quarters 2, 3, and 4, 2026 remains to be seen. I consider it highly uncertain. Although, to be fair, as I have noted in past Organogenesis articles, the company has recovered quickly and strongly from previous CMS policy discombobulations.

Organogenesis has strong liquidity resources, which could get tested

As stated by CFO Francisco during Organogenesis' Q4/2025 Call, at year end 2025 it had:

... $94.3 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash with no outstanding debt obligations compared to $136.2 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash with no outstanding debt obligations as of December 31, 2024. We believe that we are well capitalized with our cash on hand and other components of working capital, availability under our revolving credit facility of up to $75 million and net cash flows from product sales. [December 31, 2024 appears to be typo, should read 2025]

Although Organogenesis has no debt designated as such, its latest 10-K reveals that it had ~$22 million in finance leases for 2025, up from ~$2 million in 2024.

Organogenesis had positive cash flow in the years 2023 and 2024, with only a modest cash burn in 2025, as reflected by the following excerpt from its 10-K:

In a note following this excerpt the 10-K states that accounts receivable increased by $115.8 million for 2025 over 2024; this could explain why cash flow went from positive to negative in 2026 despite its ballooning revenues.

Its cash burn and cash runway going forward in 2026 are somewhat speculative given its expected revenue reductions going forward. While it provides its guidance for 2026 revenue it provides none for expected expenses.

Its cash burn will likely rise substantially. If one assumes that it will rise at least commensurably with expected revenue reductions, its 2026 cash burn would increase from 2025's ~$10 million by as much as ~40% to about $14 million.

Management provides no cash runway, but with its cash and equivalents of ~$94 million, it should have an ample cash runway going forward. As I will discuss below, this cash runway estimate has a significant risk.

There are risks to a "Hold" rating on both the upside and the downside

Upside risks

Upside risks to a "Hold" rating over the near term are not hard to find. The first, and the one most to be hoped for by all investors on the long side, is that the current threats to market growth drop away. Most significant is the situation in Iran, which has the potential to resolve with a peace deal; unfortunately, this seems unlikely as both sides dig in.

Organogenesis could also enjoy upside risks specific to it. It reports Q1/2026 earnings on 05/08/2026. If it is able to report a nice revenue beat above the $43 million expected, as stated above, that would support more sanguine expectations for Organogenesis. Ditto if CMS issues more supportive LCDs for Organogenesis's products or otherwise clarifies coverage in a positive way.

Downside risks

Once a stock starts dropping, inertia can set in, resulting in further downside, particularly in a stressed market environment, as is the case currently. However, Organogenesis does not lack downside risks specific to it. The one which stands out for me is the possibility that its Q1/2026 expenses show substantial growth.

Should its expenses grow significantly, it would increase cash burn, decreasing its cash runway. Its liquidity position offers a significant cushion protecting against expense increases; however, such increases, if reported in Q1/2026, would likely impact investor sentiment negatively.

Also on the downside is the potential that CMS becomes more restrictive of Organogenesis's products. Additionally negative is the potential that its current regime turns out to have a more lasting impact on Organogenesis, extending beyond Q1/2026, replicating in subsequent quarters as well.

Conclusion

When it comes to rating Organogenesis, analysts are blocked by its long-standing policy of reporting revenues in two broad segments, without further breakdown, as shown below:

This seems to make little sense given the disparity of revenue between the Advanced Wound Care segment and the Surgical & Sports Medicine segment. There is a historical backdrop to this. As stated in its 10-K business strategy section, Organogenesis:

... entered the Surgical & Sports Medicine market with the acquisition of NuTech Medical and its established and leading presence in placental-based products in 2017.

Organogenesis's Advanced Wound Care segment includes a wide swath of products including:

  1. Apligraf for the treatment of venous leg ulcers (“VLUs”) and diabetic foot ulcers (“DFUs”);
  2. Dermagraft for the treatment of DFUs (manufacturing and distribution currently suspended pending transition to new manufacturing facility in Smithfield, RI);
  3. PuraPly AM and PuraPly XT as an antimicrobial barrier and native, cross-linked extracellular matrix scaffold for a broad variety of wound types;
  4. CYGNUS Dual as a dual-layered amniotic membrane that promotes an optimal environment for wound healing;
  5. CYGNUS Matrix as a dehydrated placental allograft that promotes an optimal environment for wound healing;
  6. sundry other products.

It gives no breakdown as to the revenue contributions of these products. When one goes to Seeking Alpha Quant's ratings section, one finds:

Now that is some strong opinions. I am unpersuaded by either.

The Wall Street Analysts' Strong Buy is unpersuasive because it appears to be based on a single analyst whose rating has stood still for a year despite the momentous changes in Organogenesis's fortunes over the last year. As for the Quant rating, I would want to give its metrics based system more weight but for the fact that in the case of Organogenesis, it has insufficient product data to work with.

At this point, with Organogenesis's record of recovering from past CMS policy changes and:

  1. its current (03/26/2026) share price is hovering near its one year low of $2.31;
  2. recent insider buy;
  3. its ReNu regenerative product for osteoarthritis [OA] of the knee. Until recently, Organogenesis was on track for an H2/2026 completion of its rolling BLA submission as stated in the Call;
  4. resumption of sales of PMA product Dermagraft by the end of 2027 to be manufactured in its new Smithfield. RI plant.

I would lean towards a Buy rating on the stock. However, I am spooked out of doing so by uncertainties arising from the current situation in the Middle East. For the next few months I would rather hold my buying activity in Organogenesis until the situation there becomes more clear.

Accordingly, I rate Organogenesis as a "Hold".

data.title: No, Alphabet Won't 'DeepSeek' Micron, Sandisk
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Memory and storage makers like Micron Technology (MU) and Sandisk Corp. (SNDK) have been the biggest beneficiaries of the boom in model inference over the past 9 months after these two companies became the gatekeepers of the most sought-after commodity in the world—memory/storage.

But one blog post from Alphabet’s (GOOG) Google Research team a couple of days ago appears to be unraveling months of excitement that markets had for memory stocks. Google’s research team argues that their TurboQuant algorithms can slash memory usage without compromising AI inference accuracy.

Markets feared the worst, just like they did with DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) last year, and sold off shares of Micron and Sandisk, concerned that Google’s memory optimization algorithm may cause a structural reset in the ballooning demand for memory & storage.

I strongly believe this is an overreaction by markets created by unmitigated gaps in understanding the full extent of Google’s algorithm.

The current pullback is a gift for investors finally wanting some exposure, and I recommend staying bullish on Micron and Sandisk.

Easy Explainer: What Is Google TurboQuant?

Note: Readers can catch up on my recent updates on Micron and on Sandisk.

Via its official blog post, Google argues that TurboQuant optimizes memory usage “with zero accuracy loss,” promising efficiency step-ups in processing AI inference workloads.

TurboQuant targets KV cache and vector search, two memory storage techniques used during the processing of AI inference workloads. Think of the KV cache as the "digital cheat sheet" used by AI models to enrich the conversation with human users and make each conversation sound like these agents know users personally.

In the past 9-15 months, KV cache has become ultra-critical for AI models because inferencing allows models to utilize KV cache management to load prior information into a model’s context window (also known as a model’s working memory), rendering each conversation with a human user sound context-rich and personal.

In a prior post on Sandisk, I explained why model context windows were one of the key triggers for the mad dash for memory and storage, because AI models used memory products like HBM and SSDs to load context windows. Investors can see below how the size of context windows exploded by an order of 3000x between October 2024 and July 2025 even as the normal growth rate in context windows paced at ~30x per year.

TurboQuant aims to make KV cache management & usage incredibly efficient, “by a factor of at least 6x,” while achieving “up to 8x performance increase," which should be better for model context windows. Additionally, Google purports another advantage through the whitepaper on TurboQuant—that models don't need to be re-trained or even fine-tuned, a big potential win for hyperscalers and frontier model companies to save on training costs were they to implement TurboQuant today.

Markets took this news as an urgent sign that the memory trade might weaken because of the potential impact markets perceive on forward-looking memory demand.

My Take On The Market’s Knee-Jerk Reaction On Memory

Without taking away the impressive results shared by Google's research team in the whitepaper above, TurboQuant is not a brand new algorithm from Google. TurboQuant has been in the works for some time, and the Google Research team keeps updating their research with new findings.

The last time Google had updated their findings on TurboQuant was in April 2025, when the team said the algorithm could achieve “KV cache compression” and reduce inference costs.

This is also not entirely a new topic of interest. One of the other greatest companies in the world, Nvidia (NVDA), recently showcased at GTC26 last week how KV cache management was at the crux of their solution stack to mitigate rapidly rising inference costs.

At GTC26, Nvidia showed how its Dynamo software and KV cache optimization would work together to improve GPU utilization during the process of processing AI inference workloads. Ironically, shares of Micron and Sandisk rose 8% and 14% respectively after Nvidia's demonstration between Monday through Wednesday last week. Last year, SK Hynix themselves presented their solution for optimizing KV cache utilization by using CXL memory. So there is immense work being done by the industry to optimize memory utilization in AI.

My hard take on this sudden bearishness on memory stocks is quite simple. By selling off memory stocks, markets suddenly formed the opinion that the whitepaper findings of Google TurboQuant would directly arrest the skyrocketing prices of memory components like DRAM (used in HBM) and NAND flash (used in SSDs), which directly impacts the outlook of memory/storage companies like Micron and Sandisk. Essentially, markets believe the supply shocks in the memory world, which Micron and Sandisk became huge beneficiaries of, would now be alleviated.

The reality, from my point of view, is very different. Findings from Google and Nvidia are actually trying to address usage on the demand side to some extent by targeting efficient KV cache usage.

Go back to Exhibit B above and see how the size of model context windows has surged by an order of 3000x over the past many months. That’s many orders of magnitude higher than the 5x-6x increase in DRAM and NAND prices every memory stakeholder witnessed over the past ~9 months. All of this is happening due to the incredible surge in tokens being generated. Last week, at GTC '26, Nvidia’s Jensen Huang noted how “the number of tokens being generated has exploded, creating even greater need for running inference at faster speeds.”

In fact, I believe Nvidia still has a strong outlook on the memory sector. That's the only reason that explains why the GPU platform company implemented a product strategy that involved launching an entire rack-scale solution just for memory and storage last week.

To summarize my view, TurboQuant actually has a lot less to do with memory, really, and a lot more to do with inference costs and efficient memory usage, two different sides of the demand/supply equation.

The Real Outlook Of Memory Stocks

Much of the evidence, in my view, points to markets being in a "sell-the-news" mode, rushing to lock in profits on their surging gains on Micron and Sandisk shares (and other memory stocks around the world) after months-long rallies.

It isn’t really the Google TurboQuant news that entirely exacerbated this selloff. One of the triggers is Micron’s guidance on capex, like I detailed in my most recent update on Micron from earlier this week. The other trigger is possibly some sort of normalization in prices of DRAM that I have illustrated below.

Before readers jump to any knee-jerk conclusions, I want to explain two things.

First, the normalization in prices is mostly being seen in DRAM spot and not as much in NAND flash spot. Further, the normalization in DRAM prices was being seen through March, well before Google’s TurboQuant news was announced. I suspect some of the normalization in DRAM prices is happening because ​​of Samsung reportedly pausing price hikes in some DRAM chip categories, as Trendforce reports. But a pickup in DRAM prices is expected again in the next half, as a majority of the memory/storage industry has recently suggested and as Trenforce report above indicates.

Second, this normalization in memory commodity prices will impact memory companies like Micron and Sandisk in the immediate short term. I believe this only gives investors appealing entry points to gain exposure to Micron and Sandisk because the outlook for prices of the respective memory commodities that each company specializes in selling is still strong. That would directly translate to strong outlooks for Micron and Sandisk as well for the rest of the year.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

Currently, markets are beginning to price in the risk that prices of memory/storage chip commodities (DRAM and NAND flash) turn cyclically lower. This cyclical risk is something that markets are fearing, which investors must be mindful of. I just explained in the previous section why I do not expect any cyclical downturn in memory prices yet.

However, the prevailing sentiment might temporarily sour on the memory trade as many social accounts online are beginning to draw parallels to what DeepSeek did to Nvidia early last year. The souring sentiment on memory stocks could last a few weeks, so I suggest investors pace their portfolio allocation towards memory names in a manner that is consistent with their risk appetite during periods of such volatility.

The Google Research team that authored the TurboQuant paper is scheduled to present their findings at the ICLR 2026 workshop next month.

Takeaway

I believe the market overreacted to Google’s TurboQuant, causing a DeepSeek-like selloff in shares of Micron and SanDisk. In my view, this was more to do with markets rushing to lock in gains from months-long rallies.

The structural demand for memory components like DRAM and NAND flash is still very strong and should support the robust outlook for memory companies like Micron and Sandisk, making the current stock pullback a buying opportunity.

I am still bullish on Micron and Sandisk.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Market's Pendulum May Be Swinging Back To Value
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

For much of the past decade, investing in U.S. equities felt almost synonymous with investing in growth. Technology giants and other high-growth companies dominated index returns, leaving traditional value sectors, such as financials, energy, and industrials, trailing behind. But markets rarely move in straight lines forever. Recently, signs have emerged that the leadership dynamic may be shifting. Value-oriented stocks have begun to outperform growth after a prolonged stretch of underperformance, raising an important question for investors: are we witnessing a temporary rotation, or the early stages of a new market cycle?

Very Long-Term Historical Context

Markets have a way of reminding investors that leadership rarely lasts forever. Figure 1 shows the difference in rolling 10-year annualized returns between value and growth stocks. When the line sits above zero, the value style has outperformed over the prior decade. When it dips below zero, the growth style has been in the lead.1

What stands out is how cyclical these leadership regimes have historically been, as well as how the value style was quite dominant until recently. From the late 1940s through the early 1980s, for example, value stocks delivered meaningfully higher returns than growth over rolling 10-year windows.

But, as we know, the pattern began to shift in the past decade. The sustained dominance of large-cap technology and other growth-oriented companies pushed the metric decisively into negative territory, marking one of the most pronounced periods of growth outperformance on record.

For many investors entering 2026, this is the backdrop: a long cycle of growth leadership that increasingly raises the question of whether the next style rotation could already be underway.

Figure 1: Value vs. Growth Leadership Over Rolling 10-Year Periods

Source: French, K. R. (n.d.). Detail for portfolios formed on book-to-market. Kenneth R. French Data Library, Tuck School of Business at Dartmouth. Period based on data availability from June 30, 1926 to January 31, 2026. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Zooming in on More Recent Years

Now, many investors appreciate the capability to look at very long time horizons of historical data, but they recognize that there is always a balancing act because long history and a focus on what might be different about the present period. Additionally, sometimes it is worthwhile to zoom in on calendar year returns as opposed to rolling 10-Year periods.

Two of the most widely referenced exchange-traded funds (ETFs) for exposure to U.S. equities when investors are looking for the ‘value’ or the ‘growth’ styles are:

  • iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF): The iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF is designed to track the total return performance of, before fees and expenses, the Russell 1000 Growth Index. The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the large cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively higher price-to-book ratios, higher I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and higher sales per share historical growth (5 years).
  • iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD): The iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF is designed to track the total return performance of, before fees and expenses, the Russell 1000 Value Index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the US equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years).

Figure 2 is able to look back to 2015 at how these two ETFs have performed. This represents 11 full calendar years and then the period to start 2026, through February 28th. In nine out of the eleven full years, IWF outperformed, and there were only two years where the outperformance was less than 5%. In two out of the 11 years, IWD outperformed. One of those years was 2022, a well-known difficult equity market environment during a period of significant rising interest rates and inflation concerns.

One of the most important questions for U.S. equity investors involves looking at what we have seen so far in 2026. Do they want to position for ongoing outperformance of the value style, or do they view what we have seen so far as a ‘growth-reset’ and an opportunity to capture exposure to these types of companies after a bit of a lull.

Figure 2: Calendar Year Performance for IWF vs. IWD

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet and Morningstar, with data accessed on March 13, 2025. Data is shown on an NAV total return basis. For standardized performance, please look to Figure 3b in this piece. Past performance is not indicative of future returns.

Digging Further into the Value Style

WisdomTree has been managing exposures that exemplify the value style in U.S. equities since June 16, 2006. At that time, weighting companies by their dividend stream as opposed to their market capitalizations was nothing short of revolutionary. Here, we want to focus on two strategies that have been around for this nearly 20-year period:

  • The WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Fund (DHS): The WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Fund is designed to track the total return performance of, before fees and expenses, the WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Index. The WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Index is a fundamentally weighted index that measures the performance of companies with high dividend yields selected from the WisdomTree U.S. Dividend Index. The index is dividend weighted annually to reflect the proportionate share of the aggregate cash dividends each component company is projected to pay in the coming year.
  • The WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Dividend Fund (DLN): The WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Dividend Fund is designed to track the total return performance of, before fees and expenses, the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Dividend Index. The Index is comprised of the 300 largest companies ranked by market capitalization from the WisdomTree U.S. Dividend Index. The index is dividend weighted annually to reflect the proportionate share of the aggregate cash dividends each component company is projected to pay in the coming year, based on the most recently declared dividend per share.

In short, DHS focuses exposure on relatively higher ‘dividend-yielding’ stocks, whereas DLN focus on those large companies that pay out the largest aggregate cash dividends. In Figure 3a, we see both the long and short-term results of these approaches. DHS has come out at the start of 2026 with a real bang, when the value side of the equity ledger began to strongly outperform. One way to think about this is how the so-called ‘Mag 7’ dividend-payers do tend to be amongst the largest payers of cash dividends in the world, in many cases, but they do not tend to be amongst the highest dividend-yielding companies.

If the idea is to ‘avoid the Mag-7’, DHS has historically fit the bill. There is nothing that guarantees this will always be the case, but we can say that so far these companies have not tended toward the relatively higher dividend yields required for inclusion in this strategy.

DLN, on the other hand, does include those Mag-7 companies that pay dividends, excluding, as of March 24, 2026 only Amazon.com (AMZN) and Tesla (TSLA) because these firms have not yet declared an annual dividend policy. When we look at the longer-term track record in Figure 3a versus IWD, DLN does appear to be the stronger competitor, which makes sense because we know that these stocks have done well and DLN's approach has not systematically avoided them for extended periods.

True, we also see that, over most longer periods in Figure 3a, IWF was the winning performer, confirming largely what we already know—the growth style outperformed the value style in U.S. equities for much of the period we are able to show since these ETFs have been live.

Figure 3a: Analyzing the Long & Short-Term Performance Pictures

Figure 3b: Standardized Performance

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically data is from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of March 25, 2026, but showing returns for the period ended March 24, 2026 for Figure 3a and December 31, 2025 for 3b. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Conclusion: Watching the Pendulum

Market leadership rarely stays fixed for long. The past decade strongly rewarded investors who leaned into growth, particularly large technology companies that reshaped the global economy and dominated equity index performance. Yet history suggests that periods of extreme style leadership often give way to phases of rebalancing.

The early signals seen in 2026, where value-oriented companies, including higher dividend-yielding stocks, have begun to outperform, may represent the first hints of that shift. Whether this proves to be a short-term rotation or the start of a longer cycle remains uncertain. What is clear, however, is that investors who maintain diversified exposure across styles, and who remain attentive to valuation, income generation and sector leadership, may be better positioned for whatever phase of the market cycle comes next. If history is any guide, the pendulum does not stop swinging; it simply moves from one side of the market to the other.

Figure 4: Additional Information

Sources: WisdomTree, iShares. Assets under management as of March 12, 2026.

1 The source for this data is the Kenneth French data library as detailed in Figure 1. ‘Value’ refers to stocks with high book to market value ratios. ‘Growth’ refers to stocks with low book to market value ratios.

Important Risks Related to this Article

All funds are managed differently and do not react the same to economic or market events. The investment objectives, strategies, policies or restrictions of other funds may differ and more information can be found in their respective prospectuses. Therefore, we generally do not believe it is possible to make direct fund to fund comparisons in an effort to highlight the benefits of a fund versus another similarly managed fund.

DLN: There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. The Fund invests primarily in the securities of large-capitalization companies. As a result, the Fund’s performance may be adversely affected if securities of these companies underperform securities of smaller capitalization companies or the market as a whole. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

DHS: There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or regions increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post
